chunks.page_summary: This page provides guidance and good practices for preparing shielded sensor cables for Rheonics SME-TR/TRD (Outdoor Mount) transmitters and associated inline viscometers and density sensors.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Wire preparation for SME-TR/TRD (Outdoor Mount)

Modified on: Mon, 27 Feb, 2023 at 6:03 PM

Which Products are Involved?

SRV (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/)
SRD (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/)
DVM (https://rheonics.com/products/hpht-pvt-density-meter-viscometer-dym/)
DVP (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-gas-lng-density-meter-dvp/)
SME-TR/TRD (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/)

For SME-TR/TRD (Outdoor Mount) transmitter. Visit our product page to learn more about the full features.

Visit Rheonics product page: Products » inline fluid viscometer and density meter (https://rheonics.com/products/)

What is the purpose of this article?

To give guidance and highlight good practices when preparing Rheonics sensor cables with a crimp of length 8mm and terminal diameter 1mm. The terminals of the SME-TR/TRD (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/) are rated for wires of 0.2 - 1.5 mm2 i.e. AWG 24 - 16.

Wire preparation for shielded cable

1. Tools and BoM list
2. Cable Length
3. Cable strip
4. Cutting cable's shield
5. Cable gland installation
6. Strip the wire ends
7. Properly usage of crimpers
8. Preparing grounding cable
9. Hiding shield leftovers

1. Tools and BoM list
key_facts: The document provides wire preparation guidance for Rheonics SME-TR/TRD (Outdoor Mount) transmitters.
The SME-TR/TRD terminals are designed for wires with a cross-section between 0.2 and 1.5 mm² (AWG 24 - 16).
Cable preparation involves crimps of 8mm length and terminals of 1mm diameter.
The article outlines nine steps for wire preparation for shielded cables, starting with tools and BoM list and ending with hiding shield leftovers.
Related products include SRV, SRD, DVM, and DVP sensors.

Tools
• Crimping Tool
Crimping tools are used to deform materials and create connections. To connect wires together or to other connectors, crimping is commonly used in electrical work.

Figure 1. Crimping Tool - 24-10 AWG.[1]
• Precision Micro Wire Cutter
A wire cutter or cable cutter is a tool that cut wires and cables in a safe manner without damaging the insulation or conductors.
key_facts: Crimping tools are used to deform materials and create connections.

A wire cutter or cable cutter is a tool designed to cut wires and cables in a safe manner without damaging the insulation or conductors.

* Ferrule

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for preparing cables, including cutting and stripping the sheath, for attachment to SME-TR/TRD components.
chunks.extracted_text:
- Take the cable and cut the required length for your application by measuring with tape. We recommend stripping out the cable sheath at 18 cm (7 inches) from the end as a suitable length to prepare the cables for attachment to SME-TR/TRD.

Figure 12. Cutting cable to the correct length.

3. Cable Strip

Requirements:

1. Knife

- Strip out the sheath with the help of the cutter and a previous cut.

Figure 13. Strip cable sheath.

4. Cutting´s cable shield

Requirements:

1. Knife
2. Cutter
key_facts: Cables should be cut to the required length for the specific application.
The cable sheath is recommended to be stripped 18 cm (7 inches) from the end.
The stripped cable length prepares the cables for attachment to SME-TR/TRD.
A knife is required for cable stripping.
The cable sheath should be stripped using a cutter and a previous cut.
A knife and a cutter are required for cutting the cable shield.
diagram_labels: Figure 12. Cutting cable to the correct length.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for preparing a cable by removing its metal shield and disassembling a cable gland into its three main components for subsequent installation.
chunks.extracted_text:
- Loosen the metal shield from the black cable cover and cut it out.

Figure 14. Removing the cable shielding to get access to the internal wires.

5. Cable gland installation

Requirement:
1. Cable gland
- Take the cable gland and open it up into 3 parts as shown below – Part 1(Cap), Part 2(Cable holding part with packing), and Part 3(Body).

Figure 15. Cable gland-all pieces.
key_facts: The metal shield of a cable must be loosened and cut to access its internal wires.
A cable gland is to be disassembled into three distinct parts: a cap (Part 1), a cable holding part with packing (Part 2), and a body (Part 3).

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions with illustrations for preparing a sensor cable, specifically focusing on the metal shielding and its proper grounding for the TR/TRD case.
chunks.extracted_text:
• Pass Part 1 and Part 2 through the cable as shown.
Figure 16. Sensor cable passing through the cable gland.
• Open up the metal shield and cut it a bit shorter.
Figure 17. Cleaning shield remnants.
• Fold the metal shield back on the plastic insert(part-2) . That way the metal shield makes electrical contact with the cable gland and this is the grounding for the TR/TRD case.
key_facts: The metal shield of the sensor cable must be folded back onto the plastic insert (part-2).
Folding the metal shield back establishes electrical contact with the cable gland.
The electrical contact between the metal shield and the cable gland provides grounding for the TR/TRD case.
diagram_labels: Figure 16. Sensor cable passing through the cable gland.

chunks.page_summary: This page illustrates the proper technique for folding a cable shield into a plastic connector part and emphasizes the importance of ensuring contact between the shield and the cable gland.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 18. Cable shield is folded back into the plastic part.

* Make sure that the shield is in contact with the cable gland.
key_facts: The cable shield should be folded back into the plastic part.
The shield must be in contact with the cable gland.
warnings_notes: Make sure that the shield is in contact with the cable gland.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for stripping wire ends and emphasizes the importance of proper cable shield grounding through the cable gland for sensor connections.
chunks.extracted_text:
It is important that the metal shield from the cable is in contact with the cable gland so the electronics housing is connected to the sensor cable shield like in figure 20.
Figure 22. The cable shield is in contact with the cable gland[12]
6. Stripping the wire ends
Requirement:
1. Cable stripper
*   Stripping the wire at 9mm from the end with the stripping tool.
key_facts: The metal shield from the cable must be in contact with the cable gland.
The electronics housing needs to be connected to the sensor cable shield.
Wire stripping should be performed at 9mm from the wire end using a stripping tool.
diagram_labels: Figure 22. The cable shield is in contact with the cable gland[12]
warnings_notes: It is important that the metal shield from the cable is in contact with the cable gland so the electronics housing is connected to the sensor cable shield like in figure 20.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides detailed instructions and best practices for stripping wires, using a crimping tool, and handling unused wires for electrical connections.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 23. Stripping wire end.

7. Properly usage of crimpers

Requirement:
1. Crimping tool

Your crimping tool should have a nest for inserting the terminal. After inserting in the correct slot, squeeze the crimp tool firmly and release it to get a long-lasting, tight fuse.

Unused wires should be neither stripped nor crimped. We recommend protecting the ends with a shrink tube to avoid the ends making any electrical contacts.

We recommend crimping the ferrules on two sides instead of 4 sides as they work better with the terminals on the SME.

* Twist the wire and Crimp them (Crimpers) using the Crimping Tool.
key_facts: A crimping tool should have a nest for inserting the terminal.
Squeezing the crimp tool firmly and releasing it ensures a long-lasting, tight fuse (connection).
Unused wires should not be stripped or crimped.
Unused wire ends should be protected with a shrink tube to prevent electrical contacts.
Crimping ferrules on two sides is recommended for terminals on the SME, as it works better than crimping on four sides.
Wires should be twisted before being crimped using a crimping tool.
warnings_notes: Unused wires should be neither stripped nor crimped. We recommend protecting the ends with a shrink tube to avoid the ends making any electrical contacts.

chunks.extracted_text:
8. Grounding of the SME TR/TRD
The grounding in the SME TR/TRD is achieved with the ground lug shown in figure 26.

On the device display:
  rheonics (logo)
  Sensor (status indicator, green/active)
  Wireless (status indicator, green/active)
  HART (status indicator, green/active)
  Process
  V: 1234.56 | 1234.56
  D: 1234.56 | 1234.56
  T: 123.45 | 50.00
  rheonics (logo)
  (Wireless icon)
  (Bluetooth icon)
  HART (logo)
  (Cloud/connection icon)

Around the device cover: KEEP COVER TIGHT
On the sensor probe: rheonics

Figure 26. SME-TRD
key_facts: Grounding for the Rheonics SME TR/TRD product is accomplished using a designated ground lug.
The ground lug's specific location is clearly shown in Figure 26 of the document.
The device features a digital display that shows example or real-time values for V (likely Viscosity), D (likely Density), and T (likely Temperature).
The device supports wireless and HART communication protocols.
The device cover should be kept tight to ensure proper operation or safety.
